behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Extraction completeness (recall) is not a module-boundary failure — it is a quality failure in the AI reviewer's output. REAL-INFRA: No OS/subprocess dependency. LLM-DEP: Whether the extractor finds 'all' key terms — including ones expressed with varied phrasing or buried in dense legalese — depends entirely on what the real LLM produces. A deterministic stub would always return a fixed list and could never reveal a missed term. MULTI-STEP: Single reviewer step. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected — the failure mode is not at a module handoff; the extractor could be invoked in isolation and still fail to surface a buried obligation. Unit rejected — there is no deterministic algorithm to test; the extraction is the LLM call.
behaviors.test_description: Feed a real contract containing parties, dates, obligations, and penalties (including some expressed indirectly) to the live clause extractor. Assert that each known key-term category contains at least one match, and that no party name, penalty clause, or deadline appearing verbatim in the document is absent from the extracted output.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure being described is not a module-wiring issue; it is a semantic understanding failure specific to the AI reviewer. REAL-INFRA: No OS/subprocess. LLM-DEP: The word 'euphemistic' makes the LLM dependency explicit — whether the model recognises that 'the party may exercise discretion to delay performance' means 'the party can breach' is a judgment call that varies with the real model, its version, and the exact phrasing. A mock returning a canned flag list would trivially pass regardless of the input. MULTI-STEP: Single reviewer. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected — the failure does not appear at a module boundary; it appears inside the LLM call. Unit rejected — no deterministic parsing rule governs euphemism detection.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a contract clause to the live risk assessor where problematic intent is expressed euphemistically (e.g., 'at the sole discretion of Party A' as a disguised unilateral termination right). Assert that the assessor's output flags the clause with a risk level above 'low' and that the flag's rationale references the identified problematic pattern, not just a keyword match.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Compliance determination is a quality/accuracy failure, not a module-boundary failure. REAL-INFRA: No OS/subprocess. LLM-DEP: Whether the checker correctly maps contract clauses to regulatory requirements (e.g., GDPR data-retention provisions, jurisdiction-specific consumer protection rules) depends on the real LLM's legal knowledge. A stubbed checker cannot reveal gaps in the model's regulatory knowledge. Running 100 times on an edge-case clause could yield different pass/fail verdicts. MULTI-STEP: Single reviewer. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected — the wiring between the checker and the document pipeline is not the failure mode. The failure is in the checker's legal reasoning. Unit rejected — no deterministic rule-set is being tested.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the compliance checker for a specific jurisdiction (e.g., GDPR/EU). Feed it a contract that contains one clause that satisfies a requirement and one that violates it (e.g., missing a mandatory data-subject rights notice). Assert that the checker returns a non-compliant verdict for the violating clause and a compliant verdict for the satisfying clause, with the correct regulatory citation in its output.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This is a module-boundary failure: the merge step must correctly consume structured outputs from two distinct reviewer modules and produce a combined data structure that retains both. That handoff is exactly where the bug would live. REAL-INFRA: No OS/subprocess required; in-process fake reviewer outputs can reproduce the exact conflict scenario. LLM-DEP: The specific assertion — both assessments present with attribution — is a structural/data-integrity check on the merge logic, not a judgment-quality check. A deterministic test with fixed conflicting inputs can falsify it. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the merge function itself would hide the failure; mocking the reviewer outputs (feeding synthetic conflicting structs into the real merge) does not hide it. MULTI-STEP: No additional agentic steps needed. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic rejected — the merge step is described as a deterministic aggregation layer; the assertion does not require the merge to make a correct quality judgment, only to retain data. Unit rejected — the failure appears only when the merge module receives and processes outputs from both reviewer modules, not in a single isolated function.
behaviors.test_description: Construct synthetic reviewer output structs — one from the clause extractor marking clause C as 'standard', one from the risk assessor marking clause C as 'high risk' — and feed both directly into the real merge module. Assert that the unified report for clause C contains exactly two assessment entries, each attributed to its source reviewer, and that neither entry is silently dropped or overwritten.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is not in any single module; it is in the orchestration of three concurrent agentic steps and their aggregation. A wiring bug (e.g., one reviewer's result silently dropped under a race condition) only manifests when all three run and their outputs are collected. LLM-DEP: Each of the three reviewers is a real AI reviewer whose output content must appear in the final report. Stubs would always produce their canned outputs and could never reveal a dropped result under real latency variation. MULTI-STEP: Three independent agentic reviewer steps (clause extractor, risk assessor, compliance checker) execute, then a merge step aggregates — this is by definition multiple agentic steps in sequence/parallel. REAL-INFRA: Requires real LLM calls per reviewer to exercise actual dispatching and collection. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic rejected — the failure is not limited to a single AI call; it requires all three reviewers to complete and their results to survive collection and merge, which is a multi-step orchestration concern. System rejected — the failure is not about OS/subprocess behavior but about multi-step AI pipeline integrity.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real contract document to the full pipeline with all three reviewers active. After the pipeline completes, assert that the unified report contains at least one finding attributed to each of the three reviewers (clause extractor, risk assessor, compliance checker), that no reviewer's section is empty or missing, and that the report's clause-level entries cross-reference findings from multiple reviewers where the same clause was addressed.
